name: threat-mitigation-mapping
description: Map identified threats to appropriate security controls and mitigations. Use when prioritizing security investments, creating remediation plans, or validating control effectiveness.
capabilities: Map threats to preventive, detective, and corrective security controls
Generate mitigation plans with defense-in-depth analysis
Identify control gaps and coverage weaknesses
Recommend controls from a standard security library
Calculate risk reduction and implementation roadmaps
Validate control effectiveness through testing frameworks
author: sickn33
securitySummary: Static analyzer flagged 54 patterns as potential security issues, but all findings are false positives. The skill consists of markdown documentation files with Python code examples for security planning. Detected patterns include markdown code block formatting (mistaken for shell backticks), documentation reference URLs, and security terminology in example code. No executable malicious code, network calls, or dangerous operations exist.
valueStatement: Security teams struggle to connect identified threats with appropriate countermeasures. This skill provides structured frameworks and control libraries to map threats to effective mitigations.
userTitle: Map threats to security controls and mitigations
limitations: Provides planning and documentation guidance only, does not implement actual security controls
Requires existing threat identification as input
Control recommendations are generic and may need customization for specific environments
Does not replace professional security assessment or penetration testing

useCases.targetUser: Security architects designing or reviewing system security posture
useCases.title: Security Architecture Review
useCases.description: Review existing security architecture and identify gaps in threat coverage. Map current controls to known threats and prioritize remediation efforts.

useCases.targetUser: Risk managers and security teams planning security investments
useCases.title: Risk Treatment Planning
useCases.description: Create structured remediation roadmaps for identified risks. Prioritize controls based on threat impact, implementation cost, and effectiveness.

useCases.targetUser: Compliance officers and security engineers validating control effectiveness
useCases.title: Control Validation
useCases.description: Validate that existing security controls provide adequate coverage for identified threats. Check for defense-in-depth and control diversity.

promptTemplates.title: Basic Threat Mapping
promptTemplates.prompt: I have identified the following threats in my system: [list threats]. Map each threat to appropriate security controls using the preventive, detective, and corrective framework.
promptTemplates.scenario: Starting threat mitigation planning with a list of identified threats

promptTemplates.title: Control Gap Analysis
promptTemplates.prompt: Analyze our current security controls: [list controls]. Identify which threats are not adequately covered and recommend additional controls to close the gaps.
promptTemplates.scenario: Reviewing existing security posture to find coverage gaps

promptTemplates.title: Defense-in-Depth Review
promptTemplates.prompt: Review my security architecture for defense-in-depth. Current controls are: [list controls at each layer]. Identify missing layers and recommend controls to achieve layered defense.
promptTemplates.scenario: Ensuring multiple security layers protect critical assets

promptTemplates.title: Remediation Roadmap
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a prioritized implementation roadmap for these security controls: [list controls]. Consider threat impact, implementation cost, and dependencies. Organize into phases with clear priorities.
promptTemplates.scenario: Planning phased security improvement initiatives with budget constraints

bestPractices: Map every identified threat to at least one control from each category: preventive, detective, and corrective
Implement controls at multiple layers to achieve defense-in-depth rather than relying on single points of protection
Regularly review and test control effectiveness as threats and systems evolve over time
antiPatterns: Relying on a single control type such as only preventive measures without detection or response capabilities
Implementing controls without measuring their effectiveness or verifying they work as intended
Treating security controls as set-and-forget without regular review and updates as the threat landscape changes

faq.question: What is defense-in-depth and why does it matter?
faq.answer: Defense-in-depth means implementing security controls at multiple layers such as network, application, and data. If one control fails, other layers still provide protection. This reduces single points of failure in your security architecture.

faq.question: How do I prioritize which controls to implement first?
faq.answer: Prioritize controls that address critical and high-impact threats first. Consider implementation cost, effectiveness, and whether the control mitigates multiple threats. Quick wins with high effectiveness and low cost should be prioritized.

faq.question: What is the difference between preventive, detective, and corrective controls?
faq.answer: Preventive controls stop attacks before they occur like firewalls or input validation. Detective controls identify attacks in progress like intrusion detection systems. Corrective controls respond to and recover from attacks like incident response procedures.

faq.question: How often should I review my threat mitigation mappings?
faq.answer: Review threat mappings at least annually or when significant changes occur such as new features, infrastructure changes, or emerging threats. Security controls can degrade in effectiveness over time and need regular validation.

faq.question: Can this skill help with compliance requirements?
faq.answer: Yes. The control library includes references to common compliance frameworks like PCI-DSS, NIST, and SOC2. Mappings can demonstrate due diligence in addressing identified risks which supports compliance audits.

faq.question: What if I have limited security budget?
faq.answer: Focus on high-effectiveness, low-cost controls first. Input validation, logging, and access controls typically provide strong ROI. The skill can optimize recommendations based on budget constraints and help build a phased implementation roadmap.
